risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Using the random number generator in the statistical package Stata (version 9), and after stratification by geographical block (the administrative unit above the village), one author (ED) randomly selected 30 of the 134 study villages to receive intervention A and 30 to receive intervention B." Comment: The allocation sequence was generated with a computer random number generator, which is a truly random method. Although stratification was used, the method of sequence generation is explicitly described, so this meets low-risk criteria.

Summary: The paper states that villages were randomly selected by an author using a random number generator in Stata after stratification by geographical block, but it does not describe any mechanism for concealing the upcoming village assignment from those enrolling participants. This reports sequence generation, not allocation concealment, and there is no explicit central allocation, sealed envelopes, or other concealment method.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Naturally, given the context and the intervention, the allocation of villages to treatment or control was not blind." and "The study was not blind from the point of view of the villagers, who might have been motivated to attend the camp to ensure that Seva Mandir would not cancel the programme." Comment: Participants and personnel delivering the intervention knew the allocation, and the paper explicitly states the study was not blind to villagers. Because the outcomes were immunisation uptake and completion, knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect behaviour and attendance, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "Both surveys were blind: interviewers did not know which villages belonged to which intervention (or control) group." Comment: The main household outcomes were assessed by blinded interviewers (independent assessors). Although the primary outcome was based largely on maternal self-report, the assessors conducting the survey were blinded to allocation, and the paper further notes validation against BCG scar and logbook data; for the survey-based immunisation outcomes, knowledge of allocation was unlikely to bias outcome measurement because the interviewers were blinded.

Quote: "The same households were surveyed again at the end point." and "The final sample in the household surveys included 2188 children aged 1-3 years at the end point from 2898 households" Comment: The paper indicates repeat survey of the same households and reports a clear final endpoint sample by group, with no signal of differential dropout or exclusion related to treatment or outcome. Although the exact number lost to follow-up per arm is not fully enumerated here, the reported sample sizes and intention-to-treat analysis suggest attrition was not large or obviously imbalanced enough to bias the results.
